content.problemStatement:
Chef is building a robot frog, which he would like to be able to climb out of a well quickly.

The well has a height of HHH units. Initially, the frog is placed at the bottom of the well.

Let AAA denote the jumping power of the frog, and BBB denote the slipperiness of the well's wall.

The frog is initially at position X=0X = 0X=0.

Every second, the following two steps happen in order:


First, the frog will jump AAA units upwards along the wall. That is, XXX will increase by AAA.

If it reaches a height of HHH units or greater (i.e if X≥HX \geq HX≥H) with this jump, it's considered to have climbed out of the well, and the process stops.


Next, if it's still within the well, it will then slip BBB units downwards — that is, XXX will reduce by BBB.

Note that XXX cannot fall below 000, more formally, XXX gets set to max⁡(0,X−B)\max(0, X-B)max(0,X−B).
Chef hasn't decided on the jumping power of the robot frog, and also doesn't know the slipperiness of the well.

You are given integers N,K,N, K, N,K, and HHH.

Count the number of pairs (A,B)(A, B)(A,B) such that:


1≤A≤N1 \leq A \leq N1≤A≤N and 1≤B≤N1 \leq B \leq N1≤B≤N.
If the frog had jumping power AAA and the well's slipperiness is BBB, the frog can escape the well (which has height HHH) within KKK seconds.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains 333 space-separated integers N,K,N, K, N,K, and HHH — the bounds on AAA and BBB, the time by which the frog must escape the well, and the height of the well.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of pairs (A,B)(A, B)(A,B) that allow the frog to escape.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.
